data.title: UGL: A $4,200 Gold Breakout Play
data.description:
After breaking out to a new all-time high near $4,400 in October, Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has spent the last several weeks consolidating between $3,900 and $4,100 per ounce. With Gold once again moving above $4,200 as November winds down to a close, traders and investors may be looking for ways to capitalize on further price gains in the yellow metal through the end of the year. In this article, I'll initiate coverage of the ProShares Ultra Gold ETF (UGL) and explain why I like the product over other 'leveraged Gold' alternatives.

ProShares Ultra Gold ETF

I've covered leveraged funds for Seeking Alpha numerous times over the last several years. To briefly summarize my view on leveraged products like UGL, I believe they are tremendous for traders and short-term holding but not ideal for long-term investment. Given this, I rarely call these types of funds a 'buy' unless I have a high degree of conviction in a multi-month bullish trend - as was the case when I recently covered the ProShares Ultra Silver ETF (AGQ) earlier in November. That fund is up over 14% in roughly two weeks, and I suspect that move is just getting started.

Standard spot funds like the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) allocate to physical metal. As a leveraged fund, UGL primarily utilizes futures contracts in an attempt to produce a 2x return over Gold's daily performance:

Fund Comparison UGL GLD
Fund Type Miscellaneous Commodities
Issuer ProShares SPDR State Street Investment Management
Inception 12/01/2008 11/18/2004
Expense Ratio 0.95% 0.40%
AUM $892.14M $139.10B

Source: Seeking Alpha

Due to active management and contract rollover, UGL has an admittedly larger expense ratio compared with GLD at 0.95%. Both funds have roughly two decades in the market, yet GLD is far larger by AUM at over $139 billion compared with just $892 million for UGL. Since UGL is leveraged and regularly rebalanced, the fund is not something long-term investors want to hold unless Gold's directional trend is clear. Consider the performance of UGL versus GLD between July 2020 and July 2023:

During this three year period, the price of Gold didn't go much of anywhere, though it did produce a modest gain. UGL holders would have lost money over that same period of time. Because it is leveraged, UGL will amplify losses when Gold goes down, maximize gains when Gold goes up, and decay during times of sideways action. Thus, it is critical to time entries well when looking at UGL for a Gold trade.

Gold's Technical Breakout

A breakout like the one we just got in Gold is where I think it makes sense to go long UGL. Those who have followed my prior SA work may remember where I laid out what I believed were Gold's 'buy zone' and 'load the boat zone' back in late October. While we were close, we never did quite get into the 'buy zone' below $3,800.

However, what we do have is an asset that has reclaimed its 20-day moving average as it takes out its prior closing high from mid-November. You can also see in the chart above that I've drawn trend-line resistance from the October peak down to the mid-November relief rally peak. Gold has soundly taken out that trend as of article submission. Barring some sort of catastrophic reversal in the metal, my view is this is a clean breakout in a secular bull trend that shouldn't be faded.

UGL versus Leveraged Alternatives

It's important to reiterate that leveraged funds, even funds that leverage from the long side, are inherently riskier than basic un-leveraged funds or equity market alternatives like mining stocks. However, the reason why I personally like using products like UGL following breakouts is because I view Gold equities as risky for different reasons. For instance, if I believe the price of Gold is going higher (as I do) and I want outperformance over a 1:1 spot ETF or closed-end fund, I have a reasonable degree of certainty that UGL will give me the 2:1 return that I want. That is not necessarily the case with the miners for a variety of reasons:

  1. Public miner equity is often at risk of dilution
  2. Mining stocks have management performance risk
  3. Mining stocks have jurisdiction risk

It's not as big of an issue with the larger cap mining stocks, but the further down the cap rankings you go, the bigger the dilution risk is with Gold mining stocks. Especially when the companies down cap are either in discovery or growing out production. As with any equity, investors are betting on the horses just as much as they're betting on the theme or idea; this is especially true with mining stocks, where expertise in the field is essential. Finally, mining stocks also have varying jurisdiction risks to consider. Depending on where a mine is located in the world, there can certainly be mine nationalization considerations. While that may not necessarily be an overt risk today, in a world where Gold becomes a more appreciated form of monetary/fiscal collateral, Gold miners face increasing nationalization risk, in my view.

Then there's also the proof of the tape itself. If we go back to when Gold broke out from its decade-long ceiling of $1,900-2,000 back in November 2023, UGL has simply outperformed the mining sector on a total return basis:

Not only has UGL outperformed the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) over the last two years, but the fund has indeed outperformed its 2x goal against simply holding Gold. This is not to say that investors should avoid miners entirely in favor of a leveraged fund like UGL. To be sure, I believe there are tremendous opportunities available in the market in the mining space, and I own several individual names as well. For those who want mining exposure, I highly recommend Taylor Dart's research service, having previously subscribed.

Closing Summary

After more than a month of consolidating around $4,000, Gold seems poised to continue its secular rally to new highs based on strong fundamentals and a technical breakout over trend resistance. While I certainly wouldn't recommend having a larger allocation of investment capital to a product like UGL over something like the Sprott Physical Gold Trust (PHYS), I think UGL can be a highly useful product for traders or investors who want a little juice over what Gold will provide all on its own. There is a time and a place for mining stocks or mining funds. I own those as well. But for straightforward gold exposure in a secular bull, it's tough to argue against UGL for those who actively trade or self-direct investments with shorter-term time horizons. I'm initiating UGL with a 'buy.' I personally hold shares in the fund and expect to hold them through at least the end of 2025. In the event that changes, I will provide readers with an update.

data.title: Mach Natural Resources: Focuses On Natural Gas Development In 2026
data.description:
Mach Natural Resources (MNR) reported its Q3 2025 results and adjusted its guidance for 2026. Mach's Q3 2025 results were in line with expectations, although its distribution decreased to $0.27 per unit (largely due to temporary factors related to its acquisitions). Mach reduced its 2026 capex budget, but noted that it only had a minor effect on its expected production, while also boosting its distributable cash flow.

Mach may now be able to generate $326 million ($1.94 per unit) in distributable cash flow in 2026, although I'd expect at least some of that to go towards debt reduction. I am maintaining my estimate of Mach's value at $15 to $17 per unit after its results and guidance update. Mach's 2026 distributable cash flow is now expected to be around 4% higher than my prior estimate, although with slightly lower production due to its reduced capex.

Q3 2025 Results

Mach averaged approximately 94,000 BOEPD in Q3 2025 production, including approximately 19,700 barrels per day of oil production (21% oil cut). This was in-line with expectations and included approximately two weeks of production from its recent acquisitions.

Mach's lease operating expense went up slightly from $6.52 per BOE in Q2 2025 to $6.82 per BOE in Q3 2025. This was primarily due to the additional production from its recent acquisitions, which have lease operating expenses that are a couple of dollars higher per BOE than its legacy assets.

Mach reported $124 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q3 2025 and declared a $0.27 per unit distribution for the quarter. Mach's distribution was relatively low for Q3 2025, partly due to its unit count increasing by over 40% due to the acquisitions, while it only received the benefit of two weeks of production from those acquisitions during the quarter.

Mach also mentioned that it had $13 million in nonrecurring costs related to its acquisitions that inflated its cash G&A to $21 million for the quarter. Without those costs, its distribution would have been around $0.35 per unit instead, even with the higher unit count and only two weeks of contributions from its acquisitions.

Guidance Updates With Reduced Capex

Mach has adjusted its 2026 guidance in response to the relatively weak oil prices. It reduced the midpoint of its total production guidance by 2%, while the midpoint of its oil production guidance was decreased by 4%.

Mach noted that strong well performance and improved capital efficiency allowed it to significantly reduce its 2026 D&C capex budget with only a modest effect on expected production. It reduced its D&C capex budget by approximately $63 million (or 18%), although it also increased its 2026 midstream and land budget by approximately $25 million. This results in a $38 million total capex budget reduction.

Updated 2026 Outlook

Mach's revised guidance for 2026 calls for it to average approximately 23,000 barrels per day of oil production and 153,500 BOEPD of total production. Mach's production is expected to be 70+% natural gas by late 2026, up from 67% in Q4 2025, as it focuses on natural gas development projects.

Mach's 2026 natural gas production may increase by high-single digits from Q4 2025 levels, while its 2026 oil production may decrease by 10% to 15% from Q4 2025 levels. Mach's total production would increase by a couple percent based on its guidance.

The current strip for 2026 is now around $57 to $58 WTI oil along with $4.05 NYMEX natural gas. At those commodity prices, Mach is now projected to generate $1.419 billion in revenues after hedges.

Type Barrels/Mcf $ Per Barrel/Mcf $ Million
Oil 8,395,000 $56.75 $476
NGLs 7,665,000 $19.05 $146
Gas 239,075,000 $3.20 $765
Midstream Operating Profit $13
Hedge Value $19
Total Revenues $1,419

This results in a projection of $326 million (or approximately $1.94 per unit) in distributable cash flow for Mach in 2026 at high-$50s oil.

$ Million
Lease Operating $387
Gathering and Processing $189
Taxes Other Than Income $69
Cash G&A $27
Cash Interest $84
Capex $337
Total $1,093

Mach had approximately $1.1 billion in net debt at the end of Q3 2025. It is aiming for longer-term leverage of around 1.0x.

Mach's 2026 EBITDA is estimated at around $750 million at current strip. This would improve to near $800 million (before hedges) at my long-term prices of $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Thus, Mach may need around $300 million in debt reduction to get its leverage to its longer-term target.

Mach is projected to generate 48 to 49 cents per quarter in distributable cash flow in 2026 at current strip prices. If it pays a 35 cent per quarter distribution instead, that would allow it to put a bit under $25 million per quarter towards debt reduction.

Notes on Valuation

I am keeping my estimate of Mach's value at $15 to $17 per unit at long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas. Mach's Q3 2025 performance was in line with expectations, and the changes in strip prices for 2026 have largely been neutral for Mach. The weaker oil prices are offset by stronger natural gas prices since I last looked at Mach.

Mach does expect slightly lower production in 2026 than before based on its guidance midpoints, but this is balanced out by the significant D&C capex reductions that help boost its projected distributable cash flow next year.

Conclusion

Mach reported solid Q3 2025 results, along with a $0.27 per unit distribution that was relatively low due to temporary acquisition related impacts. There were nonrecurring acquisition costs, and only a couple of weeks of production from the acquisitions were included in the quarter.

Mach is focusing on natural gas development in 2026 and also tweaked its 2026 plans to reduce its D&C capex with only a minor effect on expected production. I expect it to generate 48 to 49 cents per quarter in 2026 distributable cash flow, but I also expect its distribution to be somewhat lower than that as it puts some funds towards debt reduction.

After Mach's Q3 2025 results and its 2026 guidance updates, I am maintaining its estimated value at $15 to $17 per unit. This assumes a long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas scenario.

data.title: The S&P 500's Thanksgiving Leftovers
data.description:
Would the Dogs of the Dow investing strategy work for the year's worst performing component stocks of the S&P 500 (SPX)?

We're asking that question because Seeking Alpha's Jason Capul posted an article titled "Thanksgiving leftovers no one wants: 10 worst S&P 500 stocks of 2025" just before the Thanksgiving holiday for the U.S. stock market.

Now that it's the day after the Thanksgiving holiday, we're wondering if the S&P 500's 'Thanksgiving leftovers,' the ten stocks that have lost the most value during the year-to-date in 2025, might provide an opportunity to deliver better returns than the S&P 500 itself in the next year.

Which would be cool if it works, but we don't know that it does. Here's how Investopedia describes how the Dogs of the Dow investing strategy works:

The general concept is to allocate money to the 10 highest dividend-yielding, blue-chip stocks among the 30 components of the DJIA. This strategy requires rebalancing at the beginning of each calendar year.

That presents a problem for the 10 worst-performing stocks of the S&P 500, because none of these stocks would be considered blue chip companies and because seven of the ten pay no dividends. Here's the list with stock prices and year-to-date (YTD) stock price performance as of 28 November 2025 to make them genuine Thanksgiving leftovers!

  • Fiserv (FISV) - $61.47 (YTD: -70.1%)
  • Trade Desk (TTD) - $39.56 (YTD: -66.3%)
  • Deckers Outdoor (DECK) - $88.03 (YTD: -56.7%)
  • Gartner (IT) - $232.74 (YTD: -52.0%)
  • Lululemon Athletica (LULU) - $184.18 (YTD: -51.8%)
  • Molina Healthcare (MOH) - $148.26 (YTD: -49.1%)
  • Alexandria Real Estate Equities (ARE) - $53.67 (YTD: -45.0%)
  • Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) - $34.52 (YTD: -42.8%)
  • FactSet Research Systems (FDS) - $277.27 (YTD: -42.3%)
  • Dow Inc. (DOW) - $23.85 (YTD: -40.6%)

The three firms that pay dividends on this list are Alexandria Real Estate Equities (a real estate investment trust that focuses on pharmaceutical and biotech facilities), FactSet Research Systems (a provider of financial data and analysis for financial professionals), and Dow Inc. (a manufacturer of chemicals, with no relation to the Dow Jones Industrial Average that puts the "Dow" into the "Dogs of the Dow" investing strategy).

For our Thanksgiving leftovers from the S&P 500, we're going to look at two hypothetical investing scenarios. For the first, we'll invest in only the "high-yielding" members of the list of Thanksgiving leftover stocks and compare how that investment performs against the seven other stocks in the list and the S&P 500 as a whole.

For the second hypothetical scenario, we'll invest in the entire list of stocks with the size of the investments weighted by their share of the ten stocks' market capitalizations as of 28 November 2025, and see how that compares against the S&P 500.

We have no idea how any of that is going to turn out. Will reversion to the mean happen for these worst performers, giving each a market-beating rate of return?

Or will the troubles at the companies that caused their stock prices to plummet so much in the first place keep them falling through the floor? Will the dividend-paying firms or non-dividend-paying firms deliver a better performance? Or will the S&P 500 reign supreme over all of the above?

We're excited to find out! We'll nail down the methodology and update the status of how the Thanksgiving leftover stocks are doing about once a month, with the first update coming sometime in January 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Can Platinum Catch Up To Gold? Platinum Price Forecast
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Not the most commonly traded precious metal, platinum is starting to gather significant attention from market participants as it begins to outpace its peers.

Even more scarce than gold, platinum boasts crucial industrial utility, particularly in the automotive industry for catalytic converters, as well as in the industrial, medical, and electronics sectors.

Compared to the yellow metal, platinum doesn't hold the same central bank demand as a store of value.

Nevertheless, its industrial use, rarity, and historical correlation to gold make it an intriguing asset to trade, especially amidst the yearly frenzy around precious metals.

The gold-to-platinum ratio, which has historically hovered between 1.0 and 1.5, has widened dramatically throughout the 2010s and currently stands elevated at roughly 2.50 (with gold around $4,190 and platinum near $1,640).

While gold is now back around $4,200 in another strong move higher, platinum has actively outperformed other metals in the past week, rising close to 2% on the day.

This strength is underpinned by persistent supply deficits projected to last through 2029 and transformative market developments like China's launch of new platinum futures contracts, boosting demand even further.

Let's dive into a technical analysis for this uncommon trading metal to spot what opportunities XPT/USD (platinum) has to offer.

Platinum (XPT/USD) Technical Analysis

Platinum (XPT/USD) Daily Chart

Platinum has been initiating a strong impulsive move higher since last Friday, coinciding with the drop in the dollar and the dovish repricing for the December 10 meeting.

Like other metals, platinum demand is strongly tied to interest rates (inverse correlation). A $600 move higher from April to July shaped quite a precedent in platinum, which had been left on the sidelines throughout the past year.

Interest in platinum dropped again as both silver and gold were racing to new all-time highs after August. In such commodity cycles, it is common to see lagging products in the market, which can offer decent trading opportunities.

Now, if platinum manages to reach and break its 2025 record at $1,747, odds of a measured move all the way to $2,100 form a decent probability of happening. In the meanwhile, keep an eye on the daily highs, bull channel and yearly highs to spot how the metal reacts to its technical resistances.

Platinum (XPT/USD) 4H Chart and Technical Levels

Platinum is attempting a breakout above the $1,620 to $1,650 mini resistance but is facing overbought RSI conditions, which point to some consolidation. Let's see what it needs to get started again on the 1H timeframe just below.

Platinum technical levels to keep on your charts:

Resistance levels

  • $1,620 to $1,650: Mini Resistance (breaking)
  • 2013 and current year highs: $1,700 to $1,750 ($1,747 2025 Highs)
  • 2011 highs between $1,860 and $1,915
  • $2,100: potential measured move target
  • $2,299 2008: all-time highs

Support levels

  • Hourly support at the 50-Day MA: $1,575
  • $1,500: Higher timeframe pivot and channel lows, acting as support
  • $1,250 to $1,300: Major 2025 Support
  • $1,000: 2010 to 2020 Support

Platinum (XPT/USD) 1H Chart

Platinum has rallied in a tight bull channel throughout this week, leading to the current session's breakout attempt. With many traders still absent due to Thanksgiving week, low volumes will be needed to keep propelling the price action higher.

Now forming an hourly bearish divergence, odds of a retracement are increasing again.

  • Keep an eye on the rising hourly trendline for potential pullback entries.
  • Breaking the trendline, however, could signify a larger pullback to the 200-hour moving average at $1,547.
  • Breaking and closing above the session highs maintains high breakout odds.

Next week should be key for the precious metal.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: A Textbook Value Trap
data.description:
In my last coverage of Novo Nordisk (NVO), I argued that U.S. pricing pressure on GLP-1 drugs warranted a strong sell rating. Many (value-based) readers argued in the comment section that, back then, the stock was cheap. Well, how about now, after a 20% haircut?

In my view, Novo is a textbook value trap. In fact, it perfectly resembles why I departed years ago from value investing (notice my profile name is still Deep Value Investing, which I considered to leave unchanged after my pivot to hyped up, severely overpriced AI tech stocks). On a valuation basis, the stock looks extremely attractive. I mean, it's trading at 13x next year's earnings, which, for a $208 billion market cap company in the healthcare sector, is quite unusual. In comparison, Eli Lilly is trading at a forward P/E multiple of 46x.

So, why is Eli Lilly outperforming in terms of returns? Exactly, due to its stronger growth. Novo Nordisk has a growth problem. How do I know that?

Leaving aside the Street's estimates for 2026, which suggest low single-digit growth, the market reaction on November 24 is proof that growth matters. Why? Well, if the phase 3 trial for EVOKE/EVOKE+ was favorable (which it wasn't, as it didn't show a statistically significant reduction in slowing Alzheimers over the placebo), that would have unlocked a $58.3 billion market in the US alone, according to my estimates. That would have materially lifted the growth expectations of the company, likely setting the company back to double-digit growth in the next years.

That didn't happen, and some shareholders got tired of the underperformance of this stock and decided (rightfully, in my view) to put an end to the pain, sell, and move on (hopefully to a more promising sector, like tech).

Overall, I acknowledge some of the bulls' arguments, like the volume growth of GLP-1s in the US after the pricing cuts, the international momentum in obesity care, Medicare coverage expansion, tariff exemptions, and a sizable cost-cutting program. However, I see these as partial offsets rather than a new growth engine. Therefore, until I see a meaningful driver capable of sparking double-digit top-line growth, accompanied by an improvement in profitability, I reiterate my bear case on Novo Nordisk.

Still No Signs Of Growth

Let me remind you what the main pillar of my bear case on Novo Nordisk is.

First, take a look at the chart below showing Novo's share price over the last few years:

Now, take a look at the chart below showing the annual growth of the company's GLP-1-related sales.

Call me crazy, but I see an eerie symmetry between the two charts above. Therefore, the strongest pillar of my bear case is rooted in the strong correlation between the two charts above.

Why is this a big deal?

I already discussed one of the main headwinds in GLP-1 in my last coverage. Here is a reminder:

Assuming my hypothesis, I believe both companies are under a major pricing risk, which could affect their main growth engine: weight-loss drugs. Specifically, after Trump's remarks on October 16, I believe Novo could be at risk of seeing a 70% price cut for its Ozempic drug, which, as I am about to show below, was one of the main growth drivers this year. That percentage assumes a baseline from direct sales of cash-only customers, where the current price tag is $499/month.

Since my last article, there have been several big updates in regard to this matter. Notably, the Trump administration formally announced MFN (most favored nation) agreements with Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, covering GLP-1 drugs (Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, Zepbound, and future pills).

One of the most important developments is related to the pricing of GLP-1 injections. According to the deal, the TrumpRx platform (which will launch in early 2026) will offer Ozempic, Wegovy, and Zepbound at around $350/month. Well, that's not bad, right?

There is a catch. Even though $350/month is not a massive change from the $499/month that Novo was charging from direct sales of cash-only customers, the terms stipulate that prices will trend down towards $245/month within 2 years. That's a 50% haircut from the $499/month baseline by the end of 2027.

From a margins perspective, Novo Nordisk doesn’t disclose product-level margins for Ozempic and Wegovy. However, its financials show just how margin-rich the portfolio is (or better said, was). In FY2024, Novo reported a gross margin of 84.7%, with an operating margin of 44.2%. Now, if semaglutide economics are roughly in line with the corporate average, I believe the pricing cuts could certainly have an impact on the bottom line. Here is my estimate.

FY2024 implies COGS of 15%. Assuming the same percentage for semaglutide products, we're talking about $76 per $499 unit. At $350/month (and assuming similar costs), gross margin would be 78.2% (close to 6.5% lower than the 84.7% in FY24). At $245/month, gross margin would be 68.8% (close to 16% lower than the FY24 value).

How about revenue?

Well, using the $499 cash-pay baseline, moving to $350 implies that revenue per patient per month could decline by 30%, which requires roughly 43% more volume just to keep revenue flat. Fun fact: when pricing will be near $245 in two years, you’d need about 2x more volume to maintain the same revenue.

On the positive side (if you want to call this a positive), Medicare will now cover obesity treatment (not just diabetes) for high-risk/obese patients for the first time. Here is the excerpt from the White House PR:

The other positive part of the deal is related to tariff exemptions. Both Lilly and Novo will get a 3-year exemption from Trump’s tariffs on their drugs. Now, is this going to be enough to compensate for the haircut in pricing? I don't think so. Furthermore, the deal is also touching base on oral pills. According to the terms, future oral GLP-1 pills will launch at $150/month for starter doses in the TrumpRx platform. To put that price tag into perspective, consider that it is less than half of the GLP-1 injectables. Again, on the positive side, the FDA is putting the oral GLP-1 pills on an expedited review path, so I wouldn't be surprised to see the approval of Novo’s oral Wegovy.

Speaking of this drug, Novo filed for approval of oral Wegovy earlier this year. The FDA accepted the application in May 2025, and some sources are suggesting that the FDA decision is expected by the end of Q4 2025.

The Upside

I already noted in the section above some of the upsides of the MFN deal, namely the expedited review path from the FDA, the 3-year tariff exemption, and the fact that Medicare will now cover obesity drugs for adults.

The main pillar from the Novo bulls is the increase in volume. Here is my understanding. Obesity drugs are now cheaper. Most obese Americans live in less favoured cities, where the income per capita is lower than the national average. That, coupled with the fact that co-pays are capped at $50/month, should lead to an increase in volume, which should outshadow the price reductions.

Look, on the surface, I get it. It makes sense. Lower prices should lead to more sales, especially as obesity is higher in poorer areas (in my view, likely due to the affordability of unhealthy and addictive fast foods).

However, the Street didn't fully buy in this argument.

Take a look at the Street's revenue estimates for Novo Nordisk above. Do you see the low single-digit growth projected in FY 2026? Now, compare that to the 18% yoy growth expected this year. That really puts into perspective the impact of Trump's pricing policy.

Now, take a look at the chart below, showing the Street's revisions for EPS over the last few months.

The red color in the chart above is not a positive sign, especially as it comes with higher downward revisions for dates further in the future. In other words, the Street is less positive on the profitability of the company looking at the next 5 years. Call me crazy, but that's not a company that I want to see in my portfolio.

How Can I Be Wrong

I'm just a human. I can make mistakes. Here is how I believe my bear case on Novo could fall apart in pieces.

If we look outside of the US, international operations sales grew by 16% in the last quarter, mainly driven by GLP-1 products.

In fact, two figures that raised my eyebrow were the 83% increase in obesity care and the 168% increase in Wegovy sales in the international market. In fact, Wegovy added up DKK 20 billion out of the DKK 22.4 billion in the international operations segment.

Another pillar in favor of the bulls is the market share leadership (outside of the US) in combined diabetes and GLP-1. In the last earnings call, Ludovic Helfgott noted the following:

In the combined diabetes and obesity GLP-1 market, Novo Nordisk remains the market leader in international operations with a volume market share of 68%.

Overall, I see momentum outside of the US, and the revenue mix is not overly tilted towards the US.

In the last quarter, 54.9% of sales were originated in the US vs 45.1% abroad. So, overall, I wouldn't undermine the international segment, especially in the UK, where the company is seeing decent traction in sales.

Finally, another pillar from the bulls is the recent company-wide transformation, with over 9,000 layoffs announced globally, targeting DKK 8 billion in annualized savings by the end of 2026. Overall, I think the company decided to right-size its headcount to compensate for the losses in obesity care within the US. Is this enough to outweigh the pricing cuts from the Trump deal? Again, as I've shown in the last section, the Street is not convinced of any improvement in EPS. However, I remain open to the fact that the company could surprise us with its cost-cutting program. Therefore, I would keep an eye on the progress of this program over the next few quarters.

So far, the latest profitability guidance (after the company booked DKK 9 billion in restructuring costs in Q3) shows an expectation of 4% - 7% growth in operating profit this year. If the company executes well and is able to contain costs, I believe a figure closer to 7% is likely, which will improve the sentiment around the stock.

Valuation

If there is one reason to love Novo Nordisk is due to its cheap valuation.

In my last articles, I have been arguing that the stock is a value trap, as growth is mostly muted in the near term. In fact, as I've shown above, profitability is now pressured after Trump's MFN pricing deal.

That said, even when baking in the EPS pressures next year, the forward P/E ratio is just 13x. That's 45% below the health care sector median. Furthermore, when compared to Eli Lilly's forward P/E multiple of just 46.4x, Novo looks like a steal (from a valuation perspective).

However, in my investment style, the growth of a stock matters significantly more than valuation (despite the name of my profile). To understand why, please take a look below at the Street's estimates for Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) over the next few quarters.

Based on the table above (and comparing it with the table I've shown in a previous section for Novo), I believe it is clear why Lilly is trading at a premium and why Lilly is outperforming Novo in terms of returns.

Do you see it now? Growth matters more than valuation. That's why I'm not a value guy.

Side note before I conclude: I am bearish on Eli Lilly as well, although that will be the subject of another article.

The Evoke Phase 3 Trial And My Final Conclusion

I reiterate my bear case on Novo Nordisk. Overall, I believe the selloff after the company announced that "evoke and evoke+ trials did not confirm superiority of semaglutide versus placebo in the reduction of progression of Alzheimer’s disease" is warranted. Why?

If the phase 3 trial had been successful, that would have opened a multi-billion-dollar annual opportunity, as the WHO estimates that 57 million people worldwide had dementia in 2021, with Alzheimer's accounting for about 60-70% of dementia cases. That's a customer base of roughly 34-40 million people with Alzheimer’s dementia. To put the upside into perspective, Leqembi (a treatment for early Alzheimer's disease) has a US list price of $26,500 per year. Assuming a US customer base of 2.2 million, we are talking about $58.3B in top-line potential in the US alone.

It didn't happen, and the high single-digit selloff represents the disappointment of the few growth-based shareholders. On the positive note, I believe most shareholders are value-based. In fact, price action on November 24 is clearly characteristic of value investors buying the dip, lowering their cost basis.

Overall, the main pillar of my bear case is intact. The volume growth from lower obesity care prices in the US is not materially leading to an improvement in sales. The Street expects low single-digit growth next year, with management recently downgrading top-line estimates for FY 2025. On the bottom line, things are ever worse, with a clear deterioration in EPS while the company is losing market share in the US to Eli Lilly (and compounders, according to management).

I can be dead wrong with my bear case if the company manages to cut costs and improve the EPS beyond expectations. Keep a close eye on operating profit growth for FY 2025, as I believe the company may surprise the Street if it executes on its cost-cutting plan. Will this be enough for a turnaround?

My answer is no. The company needs something more than putting a bandage over a wound.

data.title: FIVA: It Was An Excellent Year For International Value
data.description:
FIVA strategy

Fidelity International Value Factor ETF (FIVA) was launched on 1/16/2018 and tracks the Fidelity International Value Factor Index. FIVA has 126 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.56%, a 12-month distribution yield of 2.72% and an expense ratio of 0.19%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

As described in the prospectus by Fidelity, the methodology starts from a universe of large- and mid-capitalization international companies from developed markets. Stocks are grouped by country and sector, then ranked within each group using a score based on four value metrics: cash flow yield, enterprise value to earnings, price to tangible book value and price to future earnings. Scores are adjusted to remove size bias. Stocks with the highest scores within each group are included in the index.

This article, will use as a benchmark an ex-U.S. developed markets index represented by iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA), which also excludes Canada.

Portfolio

FIVA is mostly invested in large and mega caps (90% of asset value), with a focus on Europe (55%). However, Japan is the heaviest country in the portfolio (22.8%), followed by the U.K. (13.3%), Canada (11.3%) and France (10.8%). Other countries weigh no more than 7%.

The portfolio has significant exposure in financials (25.2%) and industrials (18.3%). Other sectors are below 10%. The sector breakdown is not mush different from that of EFA, as plotted below.

The fund is well-diversified, with low company risk. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 16.6% of assets and the heaviest position weighs 2.6%.

Takeaway

Fidelity International Value Factor ETF has a portfolio of large ex-U.S. developed market companies with value characteristics. FIVA is well-diversified, with significant exposure in financials and industrials, and has outperformed the ex-U.S. equity benchmark EFA. Compared to peers, EFA has a low expense ratio, but DFIV and EFV have better 4-year returns and similar volatility. EFA has performed exceptionally well over the last 12 months, compared to both the international and U.S. equity markets. Nonetheless, it is too soon to call a comeback of the value factor, which has been lagging for more than a decade.

data.title: Superior Group Of Companies: Performance May Worsen, But That Doesn't Change Things
data.description:
The last few months have just been brutal for shareholders of Superior Group of Companies, Inc. (SGC). In the middle of August of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a Buy candidate even though share price performance had been underwhelming leading up to that point. My argument here was that shares remained attractively priced and that while issues remained, especially regarding tariffs and certain one time costs, the overall trajectory for the company would likely be positive in the future. Since then, however, shares have dropped 26.2%, which is a world apart from the 2.7% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) has enjoyed. This comes on the back of lower revenue, profits, and cash flows. Near term, we could see some additional pain. But even with that pain factored in, the stock does look cheap enough to me to justify my previous bullish assessment.

Superior Group of Companies still fits

Given how short the amount of time has been since my last article about Superior Group of Companies, not much additional data has come out. In fact, the only new data that we have covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. The newest data shows that revenue for the company has come under pressure. During the third quarter of 2025, sales amounted to only $138.5 million. That's down from the $149.7 million reported a year earlier.

Naturally, this resulted in profitability taking a hit as well. But that hit was rather significant, with net income dropping from $5.4 million to $2.7 million. Other profitability metrics followed suit. Operating cash flow, for instance, plunged from $8.2 million to negative $1.6 million. We saw the adjusted figure for this that strips out changes in working capital fall more modestly from $13.2 million to $11.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $11.7 million to $7.5 million. The good news is that the revenue decline for the first nine months of this year was more modest, with sales only dropping slightly as the chart below illustrates. But still, profitability has taken a pretty sizable hit, mostly because of pain in the third quarter.

This pain for the business was driven by weakness across all three of its operating segments. The big hit came from its Branded Products segment, which produces and sells customized merchandising products, promotional offerings, and branded uniforms to its customers. Overall revenue contracted from $92.5 million to $85.1 million. That's an 8.1% hit that the company saw, with management chalking that up to the fact that sales got pulled forward into the second quarter of this year because of worries over tariffs. There were some other contributors as well, such as lower employee turnover among the firm's customers and smaller average order sizes and delayed ordering.

All of these paint an interesting picture about broader economic conditions. You see, the jobs market has been quite weak this year, with some months even reporting job losses. This means that employees have less bargaining power and are less willing to take the risk and rewarded less well for switching jobs. And the fact that order sizes are smaller and that customers are delaying orders means that there is concern about the direction the economy is heading.

Naturally, we would expect this to have a negative impact when it comes to the Healthcare Apparel segment as well. And sure enough, it did. Overall revenue contracted from $33 million in the third quarter of 2024 to $31.5 million in the third quarter of this year. This is the part of the company that produces health care apparel like scrubs, lab coats, and protective apparel and patient gowns. This decline was painful to say the least, and according to management, the drop is caused by volume decreases associated with existing customer accounts, which can almost certainly be attributable to the issues that affected the Branded Products segment. This weakness was not something new, as evidenced by the fact that for the first nine months of this year, revenue and profits for the segment were down compared to what they were last year.

We also have the Contact Centers business, which I personally think is one of the most intriguing parts of the company. In the latest investor presentation that management provided, they highlighted just how large the market opportunity is here. The Branded Products segment is worth about $26 billion when focused on the US alone, and has been growing at a rate of around 9.2% annually. Meanwhile, the Healthcare Apparel side of the business is considerably smaller $4.4 billion and has only been expanding at a rate of 0.2% per year. However, the Contact Centers business is drastically larger, with industry revenue when it comes to the U.S. only estimated to be worth $121 billion. Right now, Superior Group of Companies possesses only a 0.1% of this overall market. But over the last five years, the annualized growth rate has been 21.6%.

Unfortunately, when economic conditions weaken, it's this kind of operation that takes a pretty big hit. Revenue in the most recent quarter contracted to $22.7 million. That's 9.5% lower than the $25 million reported a year earlier. And according to management, this can be blamed on continued macroeconomic headwinds that caused client downsizing and client attrition that outpaced new customer acquisition. For the first nine months of this year, revenue is now down 3.4%. And profitability has also declined.

To be honest with you, we should expect these spaces to continue to show signs of weakening in the near term. As I have detailed in other articles like here and here, there is a good reason to believe that we are heading toward a recession caused in large part by tariffs. With labor markets weakening, you can expect the demand for the types of products and services that Superior Group of Companies offers to show weakness also.

Even with this factored in, however, management does seem somewhat optimistic for 2025 in its entirety. They anticipate revenue of between $560 million and $570 million. That compares to the $565.7 million reported for last year. On the bottom line, however, we are looking to see some pain as evidenced by what we have already experienced. I peg adjusted operating cash flow at about $28.6 million and EBITDA at $21.8 million.

In the chart above, you can see what this means for the valuation of the company. While the EV to EBITDA multiple looks impaired, the price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple is incredibly low. This is true not only on an absolute basis, but relative to other similar enterprises also. In the table below, I stacked Superior Group of Companies up against five other similar enterprises. On a price to operating cash flow basis, our candidate was the cheapest of the group. And when it came to the EV to EBITDA approach, only one of these firms was cheaper than it is.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Superior Group of Companies 5.2 10.6
Lakeland Industries (LAKE) 49.3 20.5
FIGS Inc. (FIGS) 56.8 40.0
Aramark (ARMK) 13.4 12.8
Cintas (CTAS) 35.9 26.4
UniFirst (UNF) 9.8 8.2

Valuation is one thing, but that's not the only reason I am optimistic about the company. Another reason stems from the fact that management seems intent on cutting costs. In fact, in the company's third quarter earnings call, they stressed this as a major initiative. This includes certain operational costs, but management is also focused on looking at other areas for revenue growth in its pipeline, including hiking prices and even finding long term ways to improve profitability by means of acquisitions. These efforts should eventually bear fruit. But we probably won't see a meaningful impact here until the economic climate improves.

Takeaway

Near term, we very well could see some additional pain for Superior Group of Companies and its shareholders. But given how the stock is priced, I feel very comfortable with it. Shares are attractive even if we use 2025 estimates that are depressed compared to 2024. And this is true not only on an absolute basis, but relative to other comparable firms. Because of this, I don't mind maintaining it as a Buy candidate right now.

data.title: Bad Timing For CEA Industries' BNB Treasury Transformation
data.description:
CEA Industries (NASDAQ:BNC) currently trades at a discount, with a $257 million market cap while holding about $463 million in BNB, implying a 0.72x mNAV. However, I don't view this as an opportunity, since we may still be early in a bearish crypto cycle and the underlying business is burning cash rather than helping grow the company’s crypto holdings.

The company

BNC focuses on Controlled Environment Agriculture (CEA), providing products and solutions for indoor farming, like vertical farming, and Greenhouse Farming, with a significant focus on the cannabis industry following the legalization trends in 2012. Its products included HVAC systems, cultivation facility design, and support services. However, the company was not really expanding after 2020. So in early 2025, it started diversifying operations with the acquisition of Fat Panda Ltd. on June 6, 2025, which is an e-commerce company for electronic cigarettes ((vape)), a leader in its segment in Canada and currently larger than the CEA business. Then the company adopted a crypto strategy, holding the BNB coin (BNB-USD). However, due to its size, this BNB treasury strategy made the company primarily a means for investors to gain exposure to BNB. The vape e-commerce and CEA businesses are now relatively minor segments when considering what could influence the stock price.

The treasury strategy is simple: Instead of holding cash or bonds on its balance sheet, the company raises money by selling its own stock or raising debt and uses the cash to buy and hold BNB coin. Which, in turn, could generate interest through staking and benefit from the price appreciation of the BNB coin. In a crypto bull market, this kind of company gets really attractive and can even trade at a higher mNAV, or in other words, be valued higher than the crypto it holds in its treasury. But in summary, it is a way to facilitate crypto exposure without the need for a crypto wallet.

The BNB coin is a more centralized cryptocurrency, created by the Binance crypto exchange, which is the largest cryptocurrency exchange in terms of daily trading volumes. The coin offers discounted trading fees on the exchange, facilitating transactions. The BNB coin has a burn mechanism, where Binance uses profits to buy back and destroy tokens, reducing the total supply over time. This burn mechanism is currently managed by an automated system that accounts for the BNB price and the number of blocks mined on the BNB chain. BNB launched in 2017 with about 202 million. Currently, there are about 138 million BNB, and the burn mechanism is intended to continue until the total supply is permanently reduced to 100 million BNB.

Looking at the treasury holdings, at first glance, the stock is fundamentally “cheap”, with the company trading at about $257 million market cap, while it holds about $463 million in BNB, translating to a 0.72x mNAV, according to the company’s calculation on its website. However, I do not view this as an opportunity, as this lower valuation can continue for longer, and the crypto market is currently in a bearish environment.

My view of the stock going forward is heavily influenced by what I am expecting from Bitcoin (BTC-USD) in 2026. Recently, I have written two articles and explained my view on the short-term Bitcoin price, justifying why I think prices may fall more than 80% from the peak of around $126,000 in early October. In the first article, I explained the 4-year Bitcoin price cycles, which have consistently repeated and are continuing even after institutional adoption. I also explained the economic backdrop, which may “add fuel to the fire”, or accelerate the bearish outlook, and also showed that some other commonly viewed as bullish economic indicators for Bitcoin prices could actually not provide a bullish support, like the M2 money supply and declining interest rates. In the second article, I added some on-chain Bitcoin metrics and similar price action that confirmed my view on the repetition of the cycle. But to sum up here, I think crypto prices may continue to fall through much of 2026, following similar past cyclical trends.

The Underlying Business

For this part of the analysis, I really just wanted to see if the business is profitable, if it could sustain the operations by itself, paying all the costs, salaries, and still having something left to buy more BNB coin, because, most crypto treasury companies that I have analyzed did not have profitable underlying operations, like (SBET) and (ZONE). This, in my opinion, makes the crypto-treasury strategy weaker, since the business will require cash that could otherwise be allocated to buy more of the cryptocurrency held.

The CEA business has experienced a slowdown since 2020. Revenue declined from about $13.6 million in 2021 to $11.3 million in 2022, $6.9 million in 2023, and $2.8 million in 2024. Management said in previous earnings calls that these declines were due to the weaker cannabis sector, with oversupply and declining demand, which led to lower revenue and lower backlog for BNC. Currently, backlog remains declining, from $6.0 million in 2022 to $1.5 million in 2023 and $0.6 million in the last quarter, which indicates that demand is still very low for BNC's CEA products. Because of the decline in the CEA business, the newly acquired vape business (Fat Panda) is much more significant than CEA. In 2024, before the acquisition, Fat Panda generated $28.5 million in revenue with a 39% gross margin, a 10% increase from its previous year, contrasting with CEA's persistent revenue decline. So, at this point, the vaping business is significantly more relevant, while the CEA segment is still in decline.

The most recent quarterly release looks a bit messy because it covers a transitional period from the merger with Fat Panda on June 6, 2025. The reported Q2 numbers start on June 7 and end July 31, 2025. However, the company also disclosed the “predecessor” financials, which are from Fat Panda alone for the entire second quarter of 2024.

According to the last 10-Q, the predecessor, Fat Panda, was profitable, achieving $0.7 million net income in Q2 2024, but the successor company is not, which is not a good first sign for a crypto treasury strategy. Fat Panda had a good income statement, with gross margins of about 41.7% and an operating income margin of 12.4%, leading to a 10.1% net margin after paying income taxes. However, the business combination with CEA brought increased costs and no profits. The Q2 2024 revenue for Fat Panda was $6.9 million, and in Q2 2025, after the business combination, revenue reached $7.5 million, but the cost of revenue jumped from $4 million to $5.2 million, effectively reducing gross margins from 41.7% to about 30.6%. Operating expenses increased to $7.9 million, due to one-time costs of $4.6 million for stock-based compensation, $1 million in accounting fees related to the Fat Panda acquisition, and $0.2 million in other expenses, also related to the acquisition. Removing the one-time costs, Operating expenses were about $2.0 million, which is close to the gross profit of $2.3 million. Interest expense was $0.3 million, meaning that, after removing the non-recurring items, earnings were effectively zero, which, in theory, means the business does not weigh on the treasury strategy.

However, this was only the second quarter result. Looking at the other quarters, the sum of the businesses' results would add to an annual net loss. For example, if we assume the same Q2 2024 Fat Panda net income result of $0.7 million for the subsequent Q3, Q4 2024, and Q1 2025 quarters, which only displays the CEA business results, net income would be about zero on Q3 2024, negative $0.3 million on Q4 2024, and negative $0.4 million in Q1 2025, leading to about negative $0.7 million annual net income. Although there are still changes to occur after the acquisition, and we currently do not have all of Fat Panda’s financial history, the underlying business could still achieve better results in the future. But as it stands now, the successor company is not adding to the treasury strategy.

Risks

Since I am highlighting a possible short position here, the risks are inverse, meaning anything that boosts cannabis, vaping, or the BNB cryptocurrency might drive the stock higher, possibly squeezing the shorts. For example, more US states legalizing cannabis, an increase in vape retail business, or optimism returning to the crypto space could increase demand for the stock. It is also important to note that short positions are extremely risky, since the investor could lose more than 100% of the investment.

Conclusion

Despite the company currently trading at an mNAV discount, which could become an opportunity in the future, I believe that cryptocurrencies might continue to decline throughout 2026. Additionally, the underlying business is not profitable, which demands capital that would be better allocated on the treasury side of the business. For now, I view the stock as a “Strong Sell”.

data.title: Waste Connections: Continued Acquisition Momentum, But Limited Upside Due To Premium Valuation
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Waste Connections (WCN) is one of the largest integrated waste services companies in North America, with operations in the US and Canada and significant growth in recent years thanks to numerous acquisitions, including a record amount in 2024.

WCN is down a bit from their recent peak due to the environment, and the company continues to expect solid growth ahead thanks to significant acquisitions in recent years and cost improvements, but I believe the current price still doesn’t warrant that good of an entry point to justify buying yet, although it’s a good one to pay attention to in case it continues to fall.

WCN reported an overall solid Q3 report, beating revenue and EPS expectations thanks to stronger pricing and cost control moves. During the first 9 months of 2025, we see a free cash flow of $1.06 billion ($1.084 billion adjusted). This marks a ~6% increase compared to 9M’24, but we should also note the significant increase in CAPEX.

WCN’s guidance for adjusted free cash flow was confirmed again after this report, being in line with their $1.3 billion expected, while also providing an early outlook into 2026 during the Q3 Earnings Release:

Although we do not provide our formal outlook for 2026 until February, we are able to provide a high-level framework, assuming no change in the current economic environment. On that basis, we should be positioned for the following: mid-single-digit revenue growth in 2026 from price-led organic growth in solid waste and approximately 1% revenue carryover from 2025 acquisition activity to date, partially offset by continued headwinds related to commodities.

This comes as Waste Connections reported a record year in terms of acquisitions back in 2024, with 24 acquisitions during the year (vs. 13 in 2023) that they expect to add about $750 million in annualized revenue, including deals that open new markets for them, still expanding despite their relatively huge size in the industry - being one of the largest players in North America’s waste business.

This continued to some degree in 2025, closing (or under definitive agreements) deals worth ~$300 million in annualized revenue YTD, with more expected in Q4 2025 and early 2026. This is ultimately in line with the industry’s other titans, several of which I analyzed before.

Although I like this, I’d rather see them do it using stock than debt in this environment, as that comes with a much higher cost compared to their stock’s small dividend, especially since the company trades at medium-to-high ratios compared to peers.

We’ve recently seen some more confidence from the market despite the recent price decline, with a Bernstein analyst rating Waste Connections with Outperform thanks to similar market dynamics as their leading peer, Waste Management (WM), expecting an inflection point in the latter’s free cash flow as volumes would rise and CAPEX should fall.

Financially, based on WCN’s latest report, we see an okay position, even though the current assets don’t cover the current liabilities. This is relatively in line with their peers, with little equity and a ton of goodwill as a result of all the acquisitions, but the company ultimately depends on the services they provide to their partners/clients rather than hard assets, so this is not as relevant in this industry.

Dividends & Buybacks

WCN recently increased their dividend by a very solid 11.1%, to $0.35/share, although it means a yield of only ~0.79%, which is in line with the industry’s relatively weak yields, below inflation. This represents about $81.14 million per quarter and $324.56 million per year, for a payout of ~25%, so nothing bad although the dividend isn’t crazy either. As a note, this marks the 15th consecutive annual double-digit increase since WCN started paying a dividend back in 2010.

In late July 2025, WCN also approved a buyback program, spending $442.17 million so far, with a total allowance of 12.855 million shares by August 2026. As of the Q3 report, they have ~11.76 million shares left under the authorization.

Risks & Opportunities

One of the key opportunities for a company like WCN will be rate cuts. Given the company’s debt position and recent acquisitions using debt instead of cash, we’re talking about a big potential catalyst to save some costs on future acquisitions combined with the potential to refinance at better rates. Plus, these cuts are theoretically supposed to do the opposite of what the hikes did (slowing down the economy to fight inflation), so the company could benefit from rising volumes, and its clients should also become more financially stable or potentially more prone to investments.

But again, don’t expect these to be instantly beneficial, as we’re talking about delayed effects that can take years to be reflected in the company’s financials. And that’s especially given the current environment full of uncertainty after the US Government just went through its longest shutdown in history, which made the near-term future of these cuts more uncertain, although some Fed officials signaled they see the possibility of a December cut and helping the market relax a bit - but we can’t be sure until it happens.

The issue is that this also comes at a time when the impact of tariffs could’ve been relatively more visible for the company’s clients; there’s also been a recent US-China deal that helped dissipate some of this fear. Something I noted for Republic Services (RSG) is that this type of company ultimately depends a lot on economic activity, meaning that any slowdown will continue to affect it - and I say that despite the solid results seen in this industry recently.

However, I believe Trump’s tariffs could also be an opportunity in case we see companies bringing back their production to the US. Basically, anything we do generates potential revenue for a company like WCN - meaning garbage - and businesses developing their local supply chains can generate literal tons of waste each day (think about all the paper, plastic wrap, labels, etc.).

As I mentioned before, I see these tariffs as a tool to get leverage in near/medium-term negotiations rather than a long-term thing, and the $750 billion LNG deal with the EU that’s ultimately the result of tariff-backed negotiations is a perfect example. But another effect besides these potential deals for the country could be pushing companies to reinforce/reshore their US supply chains, as companies would seek to prevent/avoid these tariffs (now or in the future) and focus on local logistics and sourcing rather than relying on other countries, which is something where companies like WCN play a key role.

And we’re now looking at quite a lot of uncertainty in the market from various factors, with the latest pressure point being the overspending and questionable profitability of Big Tech’s AI investments, which started in late October with Meta’s (META) latest earnings report, when it fell by ~9%, dragging the market down for a few days as more reports came after it. This is not the first time the market crashed for similar reasons, with the market seeing a similar crash when these companies announced them in the first place, also fearing a US recession at the time - in today’s market, fear was caused by the Fed’s late October meeting instead.

Overall, I believe that WCN’s management will have to be careful about how they want to navigate this environment, and I’d like to see them go for other sources of capital (issuing stock) instead of debt, as that’s really not the time for it. As you’ll see below, they are trading at strong valuation metrics, and buying smaller peers with their stock should easily deliver accretive returns per share even with a generous premium, so there’s not really a reason to go for debt in this environment.

Even though most companies in this industry did a great job so far even during this environment, we should remember that we haven’t really seen some “real” bad times like a proper recession recently, which is when the stock would really feel it and their ability to navigate it will be proven.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Waste Management, Republic Services, GFL Environmental (GFL), Casella Waste Systems (CWST) and Quest Resource Holding (QRHC), we see relatively average-to-high ratios compared to the other players, with nothing that screams that they’re too cheap or too expensive, although they pay the smallest dividend in the larger group (compared to WM and RSG).

Their balance sheet metrics are also in a similar position, with relatively good Debt/FCF, Covered Ratio, Quick Ratio and Debt to Equity compared to these peers. If anything, their relatively higher P/CF ratio seen in the image from earlier can actually help them deliver per-share accretion if they issue stock to buy smaller peers that come at lower ratios, so it’s not necessarily a bad thing if they use it right.

Valuation

Starting with WCN’s $1.3 billion Adjusted FCF based on their outlook, I’ll assume an 8% CAGR over the first 5 years thanks to their acquisitions and cost/margin improvements combined with rate cuts and potential benefits from tariff tailwinds, followed by a CAGR of 5% for the subsequent 5 years and a 3% terminal growth rate. I’ll go for a WACC of 6.85% (cost of equity of ~7.4% and ~3.8% cost of debt), with $8.93 billion in net debt and 257.58 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This gets us to an enterprise value of $45.06 billion, an equity value of $36.13 billion and an intrinsic value of $140.26 per share, which is below the current level.

Conclusions

Overall, I don’t see that many reasons to buy WCN today. They are cheaper, but this is very far from a screaming buy in the current market. Even for relatively high growth expectations and a low WACC, this would barely be around a fair value now (a 10% CAGR in the first 5 years and 6% in the subsequent 5 gets us to $162.91 per share). I personally prefer something like Republic Services a bit more, as the numbers on that look better overall, with similar exposure and better upside potential.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American Eagle Q3: The Controversial Campaign Will Help, But It's Not Sustainable
data.description:
Introduction

American Eagle Outfitters, Inc. (AEO) is due to announce its Q3 numbers on December 2nd after the market closes, so I thought it would be a good time to dig in a little deeper into what to expect from the report and what I am looking out for regarding the company’s perceived recovery, at least in its share price, which was driven by meme-like behavior due to its recent controversial marketing campaign.

I believe that any publicity is good publicity, but I don’t think the momentum will be sustained and help the company come out of this rut of hardly any growth.

What to Expect

Analysts expect the company to make around 44 cents in earnings on $1.32B in sales, which is around 3% growth sequentially y/y. Not much growth, but typical for these companies. Analysts cite that revenue growth will be driven by the company’s brand strength, Aerie, and other category expansions. How do these numbers stack up to what the management guided previously? The management is positive on the upcoming quarter, with consolidated comps to show mid-single digit growth, which is in line with expectations.

The company’s earnings history has been somewhat mixed in terms of sales expectations. Over the last ten quarters, AEO beat estimates 60% of the time, with the most recent quarter showing the best performance in a while, especially regarding the beat on earnings.

I expect to see a beat on top and bottom lines for the upcoming quarter, which I think will be driven by the company’s controversial campaign back in June of this year. The campaign did do much for Q2 since it came out very late in the quarter, so this quarter, we will see what kind of numbers the campaign did.

I wouldn’t be surprised if we saw a decent beat on top and bottom lines this upcoming quarter, because the management said that the campaign was quite successful in bringing in a lot of new customers.

What I’m Looking For

Looking at the company's past financial performance, there is very little to like here in my view. The company’s mainline brand has performed abysmally, to be honest. There has been a consistent decline in the brand’s top line, so it will be very interesting to see what kind of effect the recent controversial Sydney Sweeney campaign had on the brand. The reception was very polarizing, but it seems that there’s no such thing as bad publicity. The saying describes this campaign succinctly.

The management already had a lot of positives to say about this campaign in Q2, even though it had very little to do with the quarter’s numbers. This quarter will show us exactly what the numbers are for the campaign, which will influence the company’s next moves to attract more customers. The management said that there was “unprecedented new customer acquisition” nationwide, with denim selling out, and 40B impressions across channels. I just wonder how much of this will translate into actual positive financial performance.

The company’s margins have been steady at around 39% for gross margins and around 8% for operating margins. The company did take a massive hit one quarter when it had to do massive inventory write-downs in Q1 ’25. Since then, the margins recovered to their previous levels. Will there be write-downs this quarter? Most likely not, as the previous inventory write-down was described as a Q1 clean-up. However, looking at the latest inventory figures, they seem to be quite elevated, going from $636m to $718m, but that could change when Q3 comes out, especially if the campaign is as successful as everyone thinks.

What could affect the company’s margins going forward is the $20m impact on gross margins from tariffs and higher advertising spend. I’m going to be looking out for any further margin deterioration past the one that we already know.

I mentioned that analysts look to Aerie as the key growth driver, which was the case in the past, but right now, looking at the recent company’s financial reports, I see very little growth, so I’m a bit skeptical that this segment is going to be the driver of growth. I will be looking out for commentary regarding the growth initiatives in Aerie that are meant to rejuvenate top-line growth, because right now, I just don’t see it.

I am looking forward to the company’s conference call to hear about the final results and the lasting effect of the Sweeney campaign, as that will give me an inside view of what the management thinks about the future marketing efforts. I’m sure if the return on investment is large enough here, they will look to other celebrities to promote their product. It is, however, a little disappointing that the last three movies that Sydney Sweeney did were box-office flops.

Lastly, the company’s share price performed really well due to the whole controversy, igniting popularity amongst the many financial subreddits, including the meme-pushing activities subreddit WallStreetBets. I haven’t seen anything that fundamentally changed in the company’s financials that would warrant its share price breaking out of the recent decline, which is right now based primarily on the controversy of the campaign. The company officially became a meme stock in my view, and what is going to happen to the share price will most likely have nothing to do with its fundamentals. So, if the campaign numbers are not as impressive as it is expected, I could see the company’s share price returning to $10 a share.

Closing Comments

I’m not touching this until fundamentals improve and the meme-craze effect subsides, which I believe will happen over the next couple of quarters, as the controversy dies down and the company returns to its decline, unless it manages to sustain the growth momentum in both product segments, but I am not going to be holding my breath.

The short interest in the company’s shares right now is over 13%, so I expect a lot of volatility going forward in both directions, and that is just not a position I want to have my portfolio in.
